title: Reduced Dynamical Maps in Finite Temperature Vibronic Coupling Models via Choi Matrices: Numerical Methods and Applications
abstract: We present a streamlined implementation of a computational framework for constructing and analyzing reduced dynamical maps for complex system--bath models at finite temperature. The methodology is based on three established ingredients of quantum dynamics: the Choi--Jamiołkowski isomorphism for the representation of quantum channels, thermofield (TFD) purification of thermal environments, and tensor-train (TT) propagation of the resulting enlarged pure state. The reduced map is obtained from a single unitary propagation in a thermofield-doubled Hilbert space and represented in matrix form through the Choi--Jamiołkowski isomorphism. The TFD evolution is implemented in the TT representation, enabling efficient propagation of high-dimensional purified thermal states. We illustrate the methodology for exciton transfer in the Fenna--Matthews--Olson complex with site-dependent structured spectral densities represented by discretized bosonic environments. The resulting maps are used to analyze decoherence, relaxation, and finite-memory effects, and to assess the crossover to an effectively time-local description. The proposed approach provides a route to compute reduced propagators and to post-process them into memory kernels, transfer tensors, and effective kinetic rate descriptions for complex molecular systems.
summary: This paper presents a new computational framework that uses tensor networks and thermofield dynamics to efficiently calculate the full evolution of open quantum systems at finite temperatures. By representing the dynamics via Choi matrices, the method allows for the study of complex processes like decoherence and relaxation in molecular aggregates like the FMO complex. The approach also enables the reconstruction of memory kernels and long-time kinetic descriptions from short-time simulations.
main_problem: Efficiently constructing and analyzing reduced dynamical maps for complex system-bath models at finite temperature without the computational bottleneck of state-by-state propagation.
main_result: The development of a streamlined TFD-Choi-TT framework that allows for the construction of the full reduced dynamical map from a single unitary propagation in an enlarged Hilbert space.
method: A combination of the Choi-Jamiołkowski isomorphism, Thermofield Dynamics (TFD) purification, and Tensor-Train (TT) representation with TDVP propagation.
model_or_system: Linear vibronic coupling models applied to the Fenna-Matthews-Olson (FMO) photosynthetic complex, featuring site-specific structured spectral densities and static disorder.
key_observables: Reduced dynamical maps, Choi matrices, site/excitonic populations, memory kernels, and the Choi spectrum (to measure entanglement and relaxation).
important_parameters: Temperature (300 K), time window (up to 800 fs), and maximum TT rank (220).
main_assumptions: Focuses on linear vibronic models and factorized initial conditions; accuracy is limited by the time window of the discretized bath correlation functions.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows spectral density discretization; Figure 2 compares site populations with and without disorder; Figure 5 displays the decaying memory kernel; Figure 6 and 7 compare TTM extrapolation and Pauli master equation results against direct propagation.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the TFD-Choi-TT framework, details the numerical implementation using Tensor Trains and TDVP, applies the method to the FMO complex to analyze decoherence and relaxation, and demonstrates long-time extrapolation using the Transfer-Tensor Method.
why_interesting: It provides a powerful, unified numerical tool for open quantum systems to capture full non-Markovian dynamics and memory effects in high-dimensional, finite-temperature environments using efficient tensor network techniques.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Quantum noise spectral density and its interpolative-decomposition discretizations for site 1 of the subunit A of the FMO complex. The solid curve shows the continuous function from MD, while the sticks indicate the discrete frequencies and couplings employed in the TT prop- agation (50 effective modes).
Figure 3. Leading eigenvalues {λα(t)} of the normalized Choi state for the eight-site FMO model.

Figure 2. Panel a: full line, site populations pm(t) = ⟨m|ρS(t)|m⟩for the eight-site FMO model starting from an initial excitation localized on site 1; dashed line, same dynam- ics but with a diagonal static disorder σ = 100 cm−1. Panel b: Excitonic state populations, ⟨Em|ρS(t)|Em⟩, for the same model, for |E1⟩as initial state; dashed line, same dynamics but with a diagonal static disorder σ = 100 cm−1.
Figure 4. Von Neumann entropy S(t) of the normalized Choi state ρChoi(t) (left axis) and effective rank reff(t) = 2S(t) (right axis).
Figure 5. Representative components of the Nakajima– Zwanzig memory kernel K(t) reconstructed from the discrete map sequence {Φn} via Eq. (37). The y-axis is scaled by 10−3
